name: Castle Dore
description: Castle Dore, located in Cornwall, England, is a significant site in Arthurian legend, often associated with the tragic love story of Tristan and Iseult. Archaeological evidence suggests a substantial Iron Age hillfort occupied and re-fortified in the post-Roman period (5th-6th centuries AD), aligning with the timeframe of Arthurian narratives. While not explicitly named in all Arthurian texts, its geographical location and historical context strongly suggest its potential role as a royal stronghold or strategic military location during the era attributed to King Arthur. Some interpretations identify it as the 'Tintagel' of the Tristan and Iseult narrative, particularly those placing King Mark's court in Cornwall. Its earthen ramparts and ditches evoke a sense of ancient power and intrigue, making it a tangible link to the romanticized past and the enduring myths surrounding Arthur and his knights. The site's strategic importance is undeniable, controlling access to the surrounding countryside. It served as a center of power and influence, potentially witnessing pivotal moments in the shaping of the Arthurian world, lending credence to its enduring presence in the collective imagination as a place of love, betrayal, and heroic deeds. The actual castle structure was likely a motte and bailey, a timber construction on a raised earthwork, adding to its defensive capabilities and signifying its importance as a strategic military location. The connection to King Mark further cements its role in the tragic romance of Tristan and Iseult, a story deeply woven into the fabric of Arthurian lore.
subtitle: Love, Betrayal, and Ancient Power

keyMyths.title: Tristan and Iseult's Love
keyMyths.description: Castle Dore is often seen as the seat of King Mark, where the illicit love affair between Tristan and Iseult unfolds. Their secret meetings and betrayals are central to the tragic narrative.

keyMyths.title: King Mark's Court
keyMyths.description: As a potential seat of power for King Mark, Castle Dore would have been a center of courtly life and political intrigue. Many important decisions and events likely transpired within its walls.

sources.author: Thomas Malory
sources.description: A foundational text in Arthurian literature, providing context for the era and characters associated with Castle Dore.
